content.text: Incident Regulatory Notification Procedures

When a Claude-related incident occurs — an API key compromise that exposed conversation logs, a model output that caused a harmful decision, an availability failure that breached a customer SLA — multiple regulatory notification obligations may be triggered simultaneously. Missing a deadline is itself a violation, often carrying penalties equal to or exceeding the underlying incident.

This sub-subtopic defines what constitutes a notifiable AI incident under the major regulatory frameworks, provides the decision logic for identifying which regulations apply, and details the procedures for drafting and filing notifications on time.

attrs.alt: AI Incident Regulatory Notification Decision Tree

content.text: Defining a Notifiable AI Incident

Not every Claude error or malfunction triggers external notification. Regulators use materiality thresholds that filter routine operational issues from events requiring disclosure. The key question in each framework is different:
Framework
Materiality Test
Key Threshold
EU AI Act Art. 73
Serious incident: death, serious injury, significant property damage, or infringement of fundamental rights attributable to high-risk AI
Attributability to the AI system + severity of harm
HIPAA Breach Rule
Unauthorized acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of PHI that compromises its security or privacy
PHI was accessed; rebuttal presumption if not clearly low-risk
SEC 8-K Item 1.05
Material cybersecurity incident affecting the company
Reasonable investor standard; typically involves financial loss or operational disruption
CCPA / SHIELD Act
Unauthorized acquisition of unencrypted personal information
Encryption status and number of affected individuals
GDPR Art. 33
Personal data breach likely to result in risk to individuals' rights and freedoms
Risk to individuals — not all breaches require notification

The discovery date — not the incident date — starts most notification clocks. Establish a clear definition of 'discovery' in your incident response policy: typically when any employee with response authority learns facts sufficient to reasonably conclude a breach occurred.
Incident Classification for Claude Deployments
Claude-specific incidents fall into three categories, each with a different notification profile:
Category 1 — API Key or Credential Compromise
If an Anthropic API key is exfiltrated or used without authorization, the incident's notification profile depends entirely on what the attacker could access through that key:
If the key was used to read conversation history containing PHI: HIPAA breach notification likely required
If the key was used to exfiltrate conversation logs containing personal information of California residents: CCPA notification required if >500 individuals
If the key was used to send fraudulent API calls that created customer-facing harms: assess materiality for SEC 8-K if a public company
If no customer data was accessible via the key: internal incident record only; no external notification typically required
Category 2 — Model Output Causing Harm

When a Claude output directly contributes to a harmful decision — a wrongful credit denial, a discriminatory hiring recommendation, an incorrect medical triage — the notification depends on the use case:
EU high-risk AI use case causing serious harm: EU AI Act Art. 73 notification within 15 days
Credit denial based on discriminatory output: ECOA requires corrective adverse action notice to the affected individual
Medical advice error causing patient harm: HIPAA incident review (if PHI was involved); state medical board notification if practitioner liability arises
Category 3 — Availability Incident Affecting SLA
Anthropic API unavailability that causes a customer SLA breach is primarily a contractual issue, but regulatory dimensions arise if the unavailability affected a regulated service:
Banking / fintech: OCC or FDIC supervisory expectations for operational resilience may require reporting to the primary regulator
Healthcare: CMS requires covered entities to maintain contingency plans for technology failures affecting patient care
EU AI Act: for high-risk AI systems, significant malfunctions must be logged and reported even if no harm resulted
Notification Timelines and Runbook
Once the applicable regulations are identified, the clock is running. The following runbook ensures deadlines are met:
Declare incident (Hour 0): the on-call engineer declares the incident, assigns an incident commander, and opens the incident record in the tracking system.
Assess scope (Hours 0–4): determine what data was accessed or affected, how many individuals are involved, and which Claude use cases were impacted.
Apply decision tree (Hours 4–8): run through the notification decision tree to identify which regulations apply and what the deadline is for each.
Notify legal and DPO (Hours 4–8): loop in legal counsel and the Data Protection Officer before any external communication is drafted.
Draft notifications (Hours 8–24): draft regulatory notices and individual notifications in parallel; obtain legal approval before sending.
File regulatory reports (per deadline): submit to the required regulatory body using the prescribed format and channel.
Notify affected individuals (per deadline): send individual notifications using approved channels; document each send with timestamp and recipient.
Post-incident report (30 days): document root cause, corrective actions, and evidence of remediation for regulatory file.
HIPAA Breach Notification — Drafting Requirements
HIPAA's Breach Notification Rule (45 CFR Part 164, Subpart D) specifies the required content of breach notifications. Each affected individual must receive written notice containing:
Brief description of what happened, including the date of the breach and the date of discovery
Description of the types of PHI involved (e.g., name, SSN, diagnosis codes, lab results)
Steps individuals should take to protect themselves from potential harm
Description of what the covered entity is doing to investigate, mitigate, and prevent future occurrences
Contact information for individuals to ask questions or learn more

# hipaa_notification_generator.py — generates HIPAA breach notification draft

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def draft_hipaa_notification(incident_facts: dict) -> str:
    system = (
        "You are a HIPAA compliance specialist drafting a breach notification letter. "
        "The letter must comply with 45 CFR 164.404(c). Include: (1) description of what happened, "
        "(2) PHI types involved, (3) protective steps for individuals, "
        "(4) what the organization is doing, (5) contact information placeholder. "
        "Use plain language. Do not speculate about harm. Keep under 500 words."
    )
    user = f"Incident facts: {json.dumps(incident_facts)}"
    
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=800,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

# Example
facts = {
    "incident_date": "2026-06-15",
    "discovery_date": "2026-06-17",
    "phi_types": ["patient names", "appointment dates", "treating physician names"],
    "cause": "unauthorized access to API key used for clinical note summarization",
    "individuals_affected": 312,
    "remediation": "API key revoked within 2 hours of discovery; additional key rotation controls implemented",
}
# draft = draft_hipaa_notification(facts)
# print(draft)  # review with legal before sending
EU AI Act Art. 73 — Serious Incident Report

Under the EU AI Act, serious incidents involving high-risk AI systems must be reported to the national market surveillance authority (e.g., CNIL in France, BfJ in Germany) within 15 calendar days. The report must include:
Identity and contact details of the provider/deployer
Description of the AI system involved (reference to the EU AI Act database entry if registered)
Date and circumstances of the serious incident
Nature and severity of the harm caused
Immediate corrective actions taken
Estimated impact on the fundamental rights of affected persons

A 'preliminary report' within 15 days is permitted when the full investigation is not yet complete. A follow-up 'final report' must be submitted within 3 months of the preliminary report. Start with what you know, then supplement.
Post-Incident Regulatory File
Every notifiable incident must result in a documented regulatory file that can be produced on demand during a subsequent audit or regulatory examination. The file should contain:
Original incident record with timestamps
Decision tree walkthrough documenting which regulations were applied and why
Copies of all regulatory notifications filed, with submission receipts or confirmation numbers
Copies of individual notifications sent, with delivery evidence (read receipts, postal tracking)
Root cause analysis and corrective action plan
Evidence that corrective actions were implemented (code changes, policy updates, training records)
Legal counsel sign-off on the notification content

# post_incident_file.py — assembles regulatory file for an incident

import json, datetime, boto3, hashlib
from pathlib import Path

s3 = boto3.client("s3")
EVIDENCE_BUCKET = "my-company-incident-evidence"

def assemble_regulatory_file(incident_id: str, components: dict) -> str:
    """
    components: {
      "incident_record": {...},
      "decision_tree_result": {...},
      "notifications_filed": [{regulation, channel, reference, timestamp}],
      "root_cause": "...",
      "corrective_actions": [{action, owner, completed_at}],
      "legal_review": {reviewer, reviewed_at, approved: True}
    }
    Uploads the assembled file to S3 under a tamper-evident key.
    """
    file_content = {
        "incident_id": incident_id,
        "assembled_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        **components,
    }
    content_bytes = json.dumps(file_content, indent=2, sort_keys=True).encode()
    sha256 = hashlib.sha256(content_bytes).hexdigest()
    key = f"incidents/{incident_id}/regulatory-file-{sha256[:12]}.json"
    
    s3.put_object(
        Bucket=EVIDENCE_BUCKET,
        Key=key,
        Body=content_bytes,
        ContentType="application/json",
        ServerSideEncryption="aws:kms",
    )
    print(f"Regulatory file assembled: s3://{EVIDENCE_BUCKET}/{key}")
    return key
Testing the Notification Procedure
Notification procedures that have never been tested will fail under the pressure of a real incident. Run a tabletop exercise at least annually that simulates a notifiable Claude incident:
Scenario: a cloud storage misconfiguration exposed Claude conversation logs containing customer PII for 72 hours
Participants: incident commander, legal, DPO, communications, engineering lead
Inject: 'The logs contain email addresses for 800 California residents and 50 EU residents'
Challenge: identify which regulations apply, what the deadlines are, and who drafts each notification
Measure: time from scenario start to completed draft notifications — target under 4 hours
After-action: document gaps found and assign corrective actions
The most common tabletop finding is that nobody knows who the DPO is or how to reach legal outside business hours. Publish an emergency contact card for compliance incidents and verify it annually.
Summary

Regulatory incident notification for Claude deployments requires knowing which of up to five simultaneous frameworks apply (EU AI Act, HIPAA, SEC, CCPA, GDPR), running the decision tree within hours of discovery, and filing accurate notices within tight deadlines — 15 calendar days for EU AI Act serious incidents, 60 days for HIPAA breaches, 4 business days for SEC 8-K. The incident response runbook must assign an incident commander at Hour 0, complete scope assessment by Hour 8, loop in legal before drafting, and file each required notification independently. A post-incident regulatory file documenting every decision, notification, and corrective action is the evidence regulators will request during subsequent audits.
